title: Web Crawler
problemStatement:
Design a web crawler that can crawl the entire web and build a search index.

**Functional Requirements:**
- Start from a set of seed URLs and discover new URLs by parsing web pages
- Download and store the content of web pages
- Extract and follow links to discover new pages
- Respect robots.txt and crawl rate limits per domain
- Avoid crawling duplicate pages
- Prioritize important/fresh pages

**Non-Functional Requirements:**
- Crawl billions of pages
- Be polite — don't overwhelm any single website
- Handle various content types and encodings
- Fault-tolerant — resume after failures

**Scale:**
- Crawl 1 billion pages per month
- Store petabytes of web content

requiredComponents: URL Frontier (priority queue of URLs to crawl)
URL Deduplication (Bloom filter or hash set)
keyTradeoffs: Bloom filter (probabilistic, space-efficient) vs exact URL dedup
Crawl depth vs breadth — how deep to go on each domain
Freshness vs coverage — recrawl known pages vs discover new ones
antiPatterns: No politeness delay between requests to same domain
Storing all URLs in memory (won't scale to billions)
Not handling spider traps (infinite URL generation)

followUpTriggers.question: If your crawler sends 1000 requests per second to the same website, what would happen?
How do you avoid re-crawling pages you've already visited? Can you do this efficiently for billions of URLs?
Should you crawl every page with equal priority? How would you decide which pages to crawl first?

rubricFull.sampleAnswer: Multiple crawler workers pull URLs from a distributed URL frontier (priority queue in Redis/Kafka). The frontier is segmented by domain for politeness enforcement. Each worker: resolve DNS (cached), fetch page (respecting robots.txt), parse content, extract links, check against a Bloom filter for deduplication, and add new URLs to the frontier. Store raw pages in HDFS/S3. A separate indexing pipeline processes stored pages. Use consistent hashing to assign domain ownership to crawler instances. Priority based on PageRank, freshness, and domain authority.

scoringDimensions.name: Architecture
scoringDimensions.criteria: Distributed crawler design with proper separation of concerns

scoringDimensions.name: URL Management
scoringDimensions.criteria: Frontier design, deduplication, and prioritization strategy

scoringDimensions.name: Politeness
scoringDimensions.criteria: Rate limiting per domain, robots.txt compliance

scoringDimensions.name: Scalability
scoringDimensions.criteria: Horizontal scaling, handling billions of URLs

hints: Think about the URL frontier as more than just a queue — it needs to support priorities and per-domain rate limiting.
Storing billions of URLs for deduplication is expensive. A Bloom filter can tell you if a URL has probably been seen using very little memory.
How do you prevent your crawler from getting stuck in a spider trap (e.g., a calendar page with infinite future dates)?
followUpQuestions: How would you detect and handle duplicate content (same content at different URLs)?
How would you decide when to recrawl a page (freshness)?
How would you extend this to handle JavaScript-rendered pages (SPAs)?

nodes.label:
URL Frontier
(Priority Queue)
partitioned by domain

Politeness Controller
(per-domain delay queue
+ robots.txt cache)

DNS Resolver
(local cache: domain → IP
TTL-aware)

Fetcher Workers
(multi-threaded HTTP clients)
× N machines

Content Parser
(extract text + outlinks
charset detection)

URL Dedup
(Bloom Filter)
~1GB for 1B URLs at 1% FPR

Raw Page Store
(S3 / HDFS)
compressed WARC files

notes: Bloom filter sizing: 1B URLs × 10 bits/element = 10Gb ≈ 1.25GB RAM at ~1% false positive rate (safe — FP just skips a URL)
Frontier is partitioned by domain using consistent hashing so each crawler worker 'owns' a set of domains — enforces politeness naturally
Spider trap defense: URL depth limit (e.g. max 5 hops from seed), URL length cap, and query parameter normalization
robots.txt cached per domain with TTL; fetched once on first hit per domain, honored strictly
Freshness recrawl: high-authority pages recrawled every 24h, low-authority monthly; change frequency estimated from past diffs
